**Input**: Any form brief.

**Expected**: Accessibility Checklist includes all 5 required items: label association, aria-describedby on error messages, aria-required on required fields, keyboard navigation order, and non-color-only error indicators. Any missing items are flagged as TODO.

skills.name: BatchJobOrchestrator
skills.description: Designs and supervises large-scale batch processing jobs — partitioning work into manageable chunks, managing worker pools, tracking per-item progress, handling partial failures with dead-letter queues, and producing completion reports.

spec.name: BatchJobOrchestrator
spec.description: Designs and supervises large-scale batch processing jobs — partitioning work into manageable chunks, managing worker pools, tracking per-item progress, handling partial failures with dead-letter queues, and producing completion reports.
spec.purpose: Turn large, one-time or recurring batch operations — processing 10 million records, re-encoding 50,000 images, sending 2 million emails, migrating a database — into manageable, observable, fault-tolerant jobs. BatchJobOrchestrator designs the processing architecture: how to split the work, how many workers to use, how to track which items are done, how to recover from failures without reprocessing already-completed items, and how to know when the job is truly finished. It produces a Batch Job Design that can be implemented in any batch framework (Python multiprocessing, Celery, Ray, Spark, AWS Batch, Kubernetes Jobs, or a simple queue-and-worker pattern).
instructions: Receive a batch job brief: the job name, the total input size (record count or data volume), the processing function (what happens to each item), the acceptable throughput target, any ordering requirements, idempotency requirements, the target infrastructure, and the acceptable failure rate (what % of items may fail without the job being considered failed).

Design the partitioning strategy: split the input into chunks. Specify: chunk size (records per chunk), partitioning key (ID range, hash bucket, alphabetical, etc.), and whether chunks are independent (preferred) or ordered.

Design the worker pool: number of workers (derived from throughput target ÷ per-item processing time), worker isolation model (process, thread, container), resource requirements per worker (CPU, memory), and scaling strategy (fixed pool vs. auto-scale).

Design the progress tracking schema: a per-item status record with fields: item_id, status (PENDING, IN_PROGRESS, DONE, FAILED), worker_id, started_at, completed_at, attempt_count, error_message. Specify the backing store (Redis, PostgreSQL, DynamoDB) and the checkpoint interval.

Design the dead-letter queue (DLQ): items that exceed the maximum retry attempts are moved to the DLQ. Specify: max retries, DLQ storage format, alert threshold (DLQ depth > N), and the manual reprocessing procedure.

Design the completion detection: the job is COMPLETE when all items are either DONE or in DLQ and the DLQ count is within the acceptable failure rate. Produce a completion predicate as a SQL query or pseudocode.
Design the Completion Report: total items, success count, failure count, DLQ count, throughput achieved (items/sec), elapsed time, and a sample of failed items with their error messages.

Produce a Batch Job Design document with: Partitioning Strategy, Worker Pool Design, Progress Tracking Schema (with SQL DDL), DLQ Design, Completion Detection query, Completion Report template, and an Infrastructure Setup section for the target platform.

examples.title: Image thumbnail generation batch job
examples.input: Job: Generate thumbnails for 500,000 product images. Processing: resize each image to 3 sizes (150px, 400px, 800px) and upload to S3. Throughput: 1,000 images/minute. Ordering: none. Idempotent: yes (S3 upload is idempotent). Infrastructure: AWS Batch. Acceptable failure rate: <0.1%.
examples.output:
Partitioning: 500 chunks × 1,000 images each. Partition key: image_id range. Chunks are independent.

Worker Pool: Target throughput 1,000 img/min = ~17 img/sec. Per-image time ~0.5sec → need 9 workers minimum. Use 12 workers (buffer). AWS Batch: 12 EC2 c5.xlarge instances, 4 vCPU each.

Progress Tracking Schema:
```sql
CREATE TABLE batch_image_jobs (
  image_id    BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
  status      TEXT CHECK (status IN (PENDING,IN_PROGRESS,DONE,FAILED)),
  worker_id   TEXT,
  attempt_count INT DEFAULT 0,
  started_at  TIMESTAMPTZ,
  completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  error_msg   TEXT
);
```

DLQ: Max retries 3. Failed items written to s3://jobs/dlq/$(date)/. Alert if DLQ > 500 items (0.1%). Manual reprocessing: re-enqueue DLQ items with corrected config.

Completion Detection:
```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) = 0 AS is_complete
FROM batch_image_jobs
WHERE status NOT IN (DONE, FAILED);
-- AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM batch_image_jobs WHERE status = FAILED) / 500000.0 < 0.001
```

Completion Report: Total: 500,000. Done: 499,487. DLQ: 513 (0.10% — at limit). Throughput: 983 img/min. Elapsed: 8h 29min.

tests.name: Idempotency prevents double-processing
tests.input: A worker crashes mid-chunk. The chunk is re-assigned to a new worker.
tests.expected: Before processing each item, the worker queries the progress tracking store. Items already in DONE status are skipped. Only PENDING or IN_PROGRESS items (with started_at older than a timeout threshold) are (re)processed.

tests.name: Acceptable failure rate threshold enforced
tests.input: Acceptable failure rate is 0.1%. Job processes 1,000,000 items; 1,500 end up in DLQ.
tests.expected: Completion report flags job as FAILED — DLQ rate is 0.15%, exceeding the 0.1% threshold. Job status is set to COMPLETED_WITH_ERRORS, not COMPLETE. Alert is sent with DLQ sample.

skills.name: LongRunningWorkflowManager
skills.description: Designs and monitors multi-day or multi-week workflows — defining checkpoints, state persistence strategies, resumption protocols, partial-failure handling, and progress reporting for processes that cannot complete in a single session.

spec.name: LongRunningWorkflowManager
spec.description: Designs and monitors multi-day or multi-week workflows — defining checkpoints, state persistence strategies, resumption protocols, partial-failure handling, and progress reporting for processes that cannot complete in a single session.
spec.purpose: Solve the fundamental challenge of workflows that outlast any single process, session, or human attention span. A long-running workflow — a loan approval, a multi-stage data migration, a weeks-long content production pipeline, a multi-vendor procurement process — must survive restarts, partial failures, human delays, and system outages. LongRunningWorkflowManager designs the workflow as a durable, resumable state machine: every stage is explicitly checkpointed, every failure has a defined recovery path, and progress is always observable. The output is a Workflow Design Document that can be implemented in any durable execution engine (Temporal, AWS Step Functions, Conductor, Prefect, Airflow, or a custom database-backed state machine).
instructions: Receive a workflow brief: the workflow name and business purpose, an approximate list of stages in natural language, the total expected duration, the systems and humans involved, failure tolerance (what partial completion is acceptable), and the target execution engine.

Model the workflow as a sequence of named stages. For each stage: assign a unique stage ID, describe the work performed, identify the actor (automated system, human, or both), estimate the duration, classify as ATOMIC (cannot be partially completed) or RESUMABLE (can be paused and continued), and list inputs required and outputs produced.

Define checkpoints: after each RESUMABLE stage and any stage whose output is expensive to recompute, define a checkpoint. Each checkpoint specifies: the state snapshot schema (what fields to persist), the storage location (database table, blob storage, workflow engine state), and the TTL (how long the checkpoint is valid before the workflow must be restarted).

Design the resumption protocol: given a checkpoint, what are the exact steps to resume the workflow from that point? Address: idempotency (can the stage be safely retried?), deduplication (how to detect if the stage already ran?), and compensation (if a stage succeeded but a later stage failed, is rollback needed — Saga pattern).

Define partial-failure handling for each stage: what happens if it fails? Options: retry (with policy), skip (mark as WARNING and continue), escalate to human (pause and notify), or abort (halt workflow, run compensation).

Design the progress reporting interface: a structured Progress Report that can be emitted at any point showing: workflow ID, current stage, stages completed (count and names), stages remaining, estimated completion, last checkpoint time, and any open warnings or failures.

Produce a Workflow Design Document with: Stage Map (table with all stages and their properties), Checkpoint Definitions, Resumption Protocol, Failure Handling Matrix, Progress Report schema, and an Implementation Guide for the target engine.

examples.title: Multi-stage content production pipeline (2-week duration)
examples.input: Workflow: Article Production Pipeline. Purpose: produce a fully researched, edited, SEO-optimized article from a brief. Stages: brief → research → outline approval → first draft → editorial review → revisions → legal review → final approval → publish. Duration: ~10 business days. Actors: AI (research, draft), human editors, legal team, SEO specialist. Tolerance: can skip legal review for low-risk topics. Engine: Temporal.
examples.output:
Stage Map:
| ID | Stage             | Actor         | Duration  | Type      | Inputs              | Outputs           |
|----|-------------------|---------------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| S1 | Brief Intake      | Human         | 1h        | ATOMIC    | Brief form          | Structured brief  |
| S2 | Research          | AI            | 2h        | RESUMABLE | Structured brief    | Research pack     |
| S3 | Outline Approval  | Human editor  | 1 day     | ATOMIC    | Research pack       | Approved outline  |
| S4 | First Draft       | AI            | 3h        | RESUMABLE | Approved outline    | Draft v1          |
| S5 | Editorial Review  | Human editor  | 2 days    | ATOMIC    | Draft v1            | Feedback + edits  |
| S6 | Revisions         | AI + Human    | 4h        | RESUMABLE | Feedback            | Draft v2          |
| S7 | Legal Review      | Legal team    | 2 days    | ATOMIC    | Draft v2            | Legal clearance   |
| S8 | Final Approval    | Editor-in-chief| 1 day    | ATOMIC    | Cleared draft       | Publish approval  |
| S9 | Publish           | System        | 15min     | ATOMIC    | Approved draft      | Live URL          |

Checkpoints: After S2 (research pack saved), S4 (draft v1 saved), S6 (draft v2 saved). TTL: 30 days.

Failure Handling:
- S3 (Outline Approval) timeout (>3 days): escalate to managing editor, pause workflow.
- S7 (Legal Review): if flagged as low-risk, skip with WARNING logged; if high-risk and rejected, return to S5.

Saga Compensation: If S9 (Publish) fails after S8 approval, retry 3× before alerting and holding in READY_TO_PUBLISH state.

tests.name: Idempotency verified for AI stages
tests.input: Workflow resumes from checkpoint after S4 (First Draft) due to system restart.
tests.expected: Resumption Protocol confirms S4 output exists in checkpoint storage. Workflow skips S4 re-execution (idempotency check: draft_v1_id present in state). Proceeds directly to S5 without re-running the AI draft generation.

tests.name: Saga compensation triggered on late-stage failure
tests.input: S9 (Publish) fails after S8 (Final Approval) has already been recorded.
tests.expected: Workflow does NOT undo S8 approval. It enters PUBLISH_FAILED state, retries S9 up to 3 times, then escalates to a human operator with the publish-ready artifact. Saga compensation is documented as: no rollback needed for approval (non-reversible business action), only retry publication.

|----|-------------------|---------------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| S1 | Brief Intake      | Human         | 1h        | ATOMIC    | Brief form          | Structured brief  |
| S2 | Research          | AI            | 2h        | RESUMABLE | Structured brief    | Research pack     |
| S3 | Outline Approval  | Human editor  | 1 day     | ATOMIC    | Research pack       | Approved outline  |
| S4 | First Draft       | AI            | 3h        | RESUMABLE | Approved outline    | Draft v1          |
| S5 | Editorial Review  | Human editor  | 2 days    | ATOMIC    | Draft v1            | Feedback + edits  |
| S6 | Revisions         | AI + Human    | 4h        | RESUMABLE | Feedback            | Draft v2          |
| S7 | Legal Review      | Legal team    | 2 days    | ATOMIC    | Draft v2            | Legal clearance   |
| S8 | Final Approval    | Editor-in-chief| 1 day    | ATOMIC    | Cleared draft       | Publish approval  |
| S9 | Publish           | System        | 15min     | ATOMIC    | Approved draft      | Live URL          |

Checkpoints: After S2 (research pack saved), S4 (draft v1 saved), S6 (draft v2 saved). TTL: 30 days.

Failure Handling:
- S3 (Outline Approval) timeout (>3 days): escalate to managing editor, pause workflow.
- S7 (Legal Review): if flagged as low-risk, skip with WARNING logged; if high-risk and rejected, return to S5.

Saga Compensation: If S9 (Publish) fails after S8 approval, retry 3× before alerting and holding in READY_TO_PUBLISH state.

## Tests

### Idempotency verified for AI stages
**Input**: Workflow resumes from checkpoint after S4 (First Draft) due to system restart.

**Expected**: Resumption Protocol confirms S4 output exists in checkpoint storage. Workflow skips S4 re-execution (idempotency check: draft_v1_id present in state). Proceeds directly to S5 without re-running the AI draft generation.

### Saga compensation triggered on late-stage failure
**Input**: S9 (Publish) fails after S8 (Final Approval) has already been recorded.

**Expected**: Workflow does NOT undo S8 approval. It enters PUBLISH_FAILED state, retries S9 up to 3 times, then escalates to a human operator with the publish-ready artifact. Saga compensation is documented as: no rollback needed for approval (non-reversible business action), only retry publication.

examples.title: Weekly SaaS product metrics email
examples.input: Report: Weekly Product Pulse. Audience: Product and leadership team (decisions: feature prioritization, retention interventions). Recurrence: Every Monday 8am. Sources: PostgreSQL product DB, Stripe. Questions: How did MAU/DAU change? What is churn this week? Which features are trending? Format: HTML email. KPIs: MAU, DAU, DAU/MAU ratio, new signups, churn rate, top 5 features by usage.
examples.output:
Report Anatomy:
| Section         | Question Answered        | Source      | Metrics                     | Viz        | Comparison |
|-----------------|--------------------------|-------------|-----------------------------|------------|------------|
| Engagement      | How active are users?    | product DB  | MAU, DAU, DAU/MAU ratio     | Scorecard  | vs. prior week |
| Growth          | Are we growing?          | product DB  | New signups, net new users   | Line chart | vs. prior 4 weeks |
| Retention       | Are users staying?       | product DB + Stripe | Churn rate, churned MRR | Scorecard | vs. prior month |
| Feature Trends  | What are users doing?    | product DB  | Top 5 features by event count| Bar chart  | vs. prior week |

Key SQL — Churn Rate:
```sql
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FILTER (WHERE churned_at BETWEEN :week_start AND :week_end)
  / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FILTER (WHERE created_at < :week_start), 0)::float
  AS weekly_churn_rate
FROM users;
```

Delivery: HTML email every Monday 08:00 ET. Subject: "Product Pulse — Week of {{week_start_date}}". Recipients: product@company.com, leadership@company.com. Conditional: always send.

Health Monitor: If product DB query returns 0 rows, abort and alert data-eng@company.com. If Stripe API returns error, send report without MRR column and flag as PARTIAL.

tests.name: Conditional delivery rule applied
tests.input: Report should only be sent if any KPI moved more than 10% week-over-week.
tests.expected: Delivery Spec includes a conditional rule: compute all KPIs, compare to prior week, if max absolute change < 10% across all KPIs, suppress delivery and log a skip event. Alert if suppressed for 3 consecutive weeks (may indicate data pipeline issue).

tests.name: Data freshness failure handled
tests.input: Report depends on a nightly ETL that sometimes runs late.
tests.expected: Health Monitor includes a freshness check: before computing metrics, verify source table last_updated timestamp is within 26 hours of report run time. If stale, hold delivery for up to 2 hours, then send with a DATA FRESHNESS WARNING banner and alert the data engineering team.

skills.name: PeriodicTaskScheduler
skills.description: Designs and documents recurring task schedules — defining triggers (cron expressions, calendar rules, event-based), execution windows, retry policies, dependency chains, and monitoring requirements for any periodic workflow.

spec.name: PeriodicTaskScheduler
spec.description: Designs and documents recurring task schedules — defining triggers (cron expressions, calendar rules, event-based), execution windows, retry policies, dependency chains, and monitoring requirements for any periodic workflow.
spec.purpose: Translate human-described recurring tasks (nightly backups, weekly reports, monthly billing runs, quarterly audits) into precise, implementation-ready schedule specifications. A good schedule definition is more than a cron expression — it defines what happens if the task fails, what happens if it runs late, what it depends on, who is notified, and how to verify it completed successfully. PeriodicTaskScheduler produces a complete Schedule Specification that a developer, DevOps engineer, or automation platform can implement directly in any scheduler (cron, Kubernetes CronJob, AWS EventBridge, Airflow, GitHub Actions, Celery Beat, etc.).
instructions: Receive a schedule brief: the task name and description, the desired recurrence (human language: every night, every Monday at 9am, first of the month, etc.), the timezone, the estimated execution duration, the acceptable execution window (how late can it run before it is considered a failure), any predecessor tasks that must complete first, and the target scheduler platform.

Translate the recurrence into a formal trigger definition: a cron expression (5-field standard or 6-field with seconds), a human-readable explanation of the cron, and a list of the next 5 scheduled execution times as a sanity check.
Define the execution window: start time, deadline (latest acceptable completion time), and what happens if the deadline is missed (skip, alert-only, alert-and-retry).

Design the retry policy: maximum retry attempts, backoff strategy (immediate, fixed interval, exponential with jitter), retry conditions (on any failure, on specific exit codes only), and maximum total retry window.
Define dependency rules: list predecessor tasks by name, specify whether this task should wait for all predecessors to succeed, skip if any predecessor fails, or run regardless.

Define success criteria: expected exit code, expected output artifact or side effect (a file exists, a database row updated, an email sent), and a health check query or assertion to verify completion.
Define alerting: who is notified on failure, on missed deadline, on consecutive failures (specify N), and the notification channel (email, Slack, PagerDuty, webhook).

Produce a Schedule Specification document with: Trigger Definition (cron + next 5 runs), Execution Window, Retry Policy, Dependency Map, Success Criteria, Alerting Rules, and a Platform Implementation block (ready-to-paste config for the target platform).

examples.title: Nightly database backup job
examples.input: Task: full PostgreSQL database backup. Recurrence: every night at 2:30am. Timezone: America/New_York. Duration: ~20 minutes. Window: must finish by 4am. Predecessors: none. Platform: Kubernetes CronJob. Notify: ops-team@company.com on failure.
examples.output:
Trigger Definition:
- Cron: 30 2 * * * (America/New_York)
- Explanation: At 02:30, every day
- Next 5 runs: Mon Jun 16 02:30, Tue Jun 17 02:30, Wed Jun 18 02:30, Thu Jun 19 02:30, Fri Jun 20 02:30

Execution Window:
- Start: 02:30 ET. Deadline: 04:00 ET (90-minute window). On missed deadline: alert ops-team, skip (do not retry after deadline).

Retry Policy:
- Max attempts: 3. Backoff: exponential (2min, 4min, 8min). Retry on: non-zero exit code. Max retry window: 30 minutes.

Success Criteria:
- Exit code 0. Backup file exists at s3://backups/db/$(date +%Y-%m-%d).pgdump. Size > 100MB (sanity check).

Alerting:
- On failure: email ops-team@company.com immediately. On 3 consecutive failures: PagerDuty P2 alert.

Kubernetes CronJob:
```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: postgres-backup
spec:
  schedule: "30 2 * * *"
  timeZone: "America/New_York"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  startingDeadlineSeconds: 5400
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      backoffLimit: 3
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: postgres-backup:latest
```

tests.name: Cron expression validated with next-5-runs sanity check
tests.input: Recurrence: every 15th of the month at noon.
tests.expected: Cron: 0 12 15 * *. Next 5 runs list the 15th of the next 5 months at 12:00:00 in the specified timezone. If the 15th has already passed this month, the first run listed is next month.

tests.name: Dependency deadlock detected
tests.input: Task A depends on Task B; Task B depends on Task A.
tests.expected: Schedule Specification flags a CIRCULAR DEPENDENCY error between Task A and Task B. Neither task is scheduled until the cycle is broken. A resolution suggestion is provided.

skills.name: DocumentTemplateArchitect
skills.description: Designs reusable master page templates, paragraph styles, character styles, and object styles for desktop publishing applications — creating the structural skeleton that ensures visual consistency across long or multi-author documents.

spec.name: DocumentTemplateArchitect
spec.description: Designs reusable master page templates, paragraph styles, character styles, and object styles for desktop publishing applications — creating the structural skeleton that ensures visual consistency across long or multi-author documents.
spec.purpose: Eliminate inconsistency and manual reformatting in long or multi-author DTP documents by designing a comprehensive template system before content is written. A well-designed template system means that applying a paragraph style is all that is needed to correctly format any element — typeface, size, leading, space before/after, color, and indentation all resolve automatically. DocumentTemplateArchitect plans and specifies the full hierarchy of master pages, style sheets, and object libraries that a DTP application needs, producing a specification that can be implemented in InDesign, Affinity Publisher, Scribus, or QuarkXPress.
instructions: Receive a template brief: document type (book, report, magazine, catalog, manual, newsletter), approximate page count, number of distinct content types, number of authors or editors, target DTP application, and any existing brand or style constraints.

Design the Master Page hierarchy: identify all master page variants needed (e.g., A-Master: blank, B-Master: chapter opener, C-Master: body page left, D-Master: body page right, E-Master: full-bleed image spread). For each master, specify: headers and footers content and position, running heads/feet variables, automatic page numbering position and style, any recurring graphic elements (rules, borders, color bands), and which other master it is based on.

Design the Paragraph Style Sheet: list every distinct paragraph type in the document. For each style, specify: based-on style (for inheritance), next style (for auto-flow), typeface, size, leading, space before, space after, indentation (first line, left, right), alignment, color, hyphenation rules, keep options (keep with next N lines, keep together), and any tab stops.

Design the Character Style Sheet: list character-level overrides needed (bold, italic, links, code spans, superscript, caption labels, drop caps). Each style is a delta from its parent paragraph style.

Design Object Styles: specify styles for recurring frame types — body text frame, image frame, caption frame, sidebar box, callout box, folio. Each style includes frame dimensions/proportions, fill/stroke, text inset, and vertical justification.
Define the Table of Contents style mapping (which paragraph styles feed into the TOC and at which level).

Produce a Template Specification document with: Master Page Map (diagram), Paragraph Style Sheet table, Character Style Sheet table, Object Style table, TOC mapping, and an Implementation Checklist (step-by-step instructions for building the template in the target application).

examples.title: Technical manual template (InDesign)
examples.input: Document Type: Technical user manual. Pages: ~200. Content: chapter titles, section heads, body text, warnings, notes, code blocks, numbered procedures, figures with captions, index. Authors: 4 technical writers. DTP App: Adobe InDesign CC. Brand: IBM Plex Sans family, color #0F62FE (IBM Blue).
examples.output:
Master Pages:
- A-Master (base): empty, auto page number bottom-center in IBM Plex Sans 8pt/Gray 70
- B-Master (chapter opener): full-width blue band top 40mm, chapter number in 80pt light, title in 36pt, no header
- C-Master (body left): header left = chapter name variable, folio left-aligned
- D-Master (body right): header right = section name variable, folio right-aligned

Paragraph Styles (excerpt):
| Style Name        | Based On    | Size/Lead | Space B/A | Notes                          |
|-------------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| Chapter Title     | None        | 36/40pt   | 0/24pt    | IBM Plex Sans Light, Blue      |
| Section Head      | Chapter Title| 18/24pt  | 18/8pt    | IBM Plex Sans Medium, Black    |
| Body Text         | None        | 10/15pt   | 0/0pt     | IBM Plex Sans Regular, indent 0|
| Warning Box       | Body Text   | 9/14pt    | 6/6pt     | Italic, amber left border rule |
| Code Block        | None        | 9/14pt    | 4/4pt     | IBM Plex Mono, gray fill box   |
| Numbered Step     | Body Text   | 10/15pt   | 2/2pt     | Auto-number list, indent 8mm   |

TOC Mapping: Chapter Title → Level 1, Section Head → Level 2
Implementation: New doc → set master pages in order B→C→D. Build styles in dependency order (Body Text first, then all based-on styles).

tests.name: Style inheritance chain is valid
tests.input: Paragraph style Warning is based on Body Text, which is based on None.
tests.expected: Template Spec shows the chain: None → Body Text → Warning. No circular dependencies. Warning inherits all Body Text properties and only overrides what is explicitly different.

tests.name: All content types have a corresponding style
tests.input: Brief lists 8 content types; template spec only defines styles for 6.
tests.expected: Template Spec flags the 2 missing content types as UNDEFINED STYLE and prompts for the information needed to define them before implementation.

- B-Master (chapter opener): full-width blue band top 40mm, chapter number in 80pt light, title in 36pt, no header
- C-Master (body left): header left = chapter name variable, folio left-aligned
- D-Master (body right): header right = section name variable, folio right-aligned

Paragraph Styles (excerpt):
| Style Name        | Based On    | Size/Lead | Space B/A | Notes                          |
|-------------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| Chapter Title     | None        | 36/40pt   | 0/24pt    | IBM Plex Sans Light, Blue      |
| Section Head      | Chapter Title| 18/24pt  | 18/8pt    | IBM Plex Sans Medium, Black    |
| Body Text         | None        | 10/15pt   | 0/0pt     | IBM Plex Sans Regular, indent 0|
| Warning Box       | Body Text   | 9/14pt    | 6/6pt     | Italic, amber left border rule |
| Code Block        | None        | 9/14pt    | 4/4pt     | IBM Plex Mono, gray fill box   |
| Numbered Step     | Body Text   | 10/15pt   | 2/2pt     | Auto-number list, indent 8mm   |

TOC Mapping: Chapter Title → Level 1, Section Head → Level 2
Implementation: New doc → set master pages in order B→C→D. Build styles in dependency order (Body Text first, then all based-on styles).

## Tests

### Style inheritance chain is valid
**Input**: Paragraph style Warning is based on Body Text, which is based on None.

**Expected**: Template Spec shows the chain: None → Body Text → Warning. No circular dependencies. Warning inherits all Body Text properties and only overrides what is explicitly different.

### All content types have a corresponding style
**Input**: Brief lists 8 content types; template spec only defines styles for 6.

**Expected**: Template Spec flags the 2 missing content types as UNDEFINED STYLE and prompts for the information needed to define them before implementation.

# BrandStyleGuideGenerator

## Purpose
Transform raw brand assets and decisions into a comprehensive, usable style guide that enforces visual consistency across every touchpoint — from business cards to websites, from social posts to packaging. A style guide is both a rulebook and an education tool: it must explain not just what to do but why, so that anyone working with the brand — internal teams, freelancers, print vendors, developers — makes decisions that feel like they came from the same mind.

## Instructions
1. Receive a brand brief: brand name, industry, brand personality descriptors (3–5 adjectives), existing assets (logo files, current colors, any approved fonts), target audiences, and primary touchpoints (print, digital, packaging, environmental, etc.).

3. Section 2 — Logo System: specify primary logo, secondary/stacked variant, icon-only mark, and wordmark. 4. Section 3 — Color Palette: define primary, secondary, and neutral palette. For each color: name, HEX, RGB, CMYK, and Pantone equivalent. Specify which combinations are approved and which are prohibited (accessibility failures, brand clashes). 5. Section 4 — Typography: reference or embed the full type system (compatible with TypographySystemBuilder output), specifying approved fonts, hierarchy levels, and usage rules.
6. Section 5 — Imagery & Iconography: describe the photographic direction (subject matter, mood, lighting style, color treatment, what to avoid), illustration style if applicable, and icon style rules (stroke weight, corner radius, grid size, fill vs outline).
7. Section 6 — Voice & Tone: define the brand voice in 3–4 attributes. For each, provide a DO/DON-T example showing the contrast. Specify tone adjustments for different contexts (social vs formal correspondence vs error messages).

skills.name: PrintPrepressChecker
skills.description: Performs a comprehensive pre-press readiness audit on a desktop publishing document — checking bleeds, color modes, image resolution, font embedding, overprint settings, ink density, and PDF/X compliance before sending to a print vendor.

spec.name: PrintPrepressChecker
spec.description: Performs a comprehensive pre-press readiness audit on a desktop publishing document — checking bleeds, color modes, image resolution, font embedding, overprint settings, ink density, and PDF/X compliance before sending to a print vendor.
spec.purpose: Prevent costly reprints and production delays by systematically verifying that a document meets all technical requirements before it reaches the print vendor. Pre-press errors — RGB images in a CMYK job, missing bleeds, fonts not embedded, total ink density over 300% — are invisible on screen but catastrophic in print. PrintPrepressChecker acts as an expert preflight operator, auditing a document description or exported file manifest against a configurable print specification, and producing a Preflight Report that a designer can act on immediately.
instructions: Receive a preflight brief: the document description (dimensions, page count, color intent, image list with modes and resolutions, font list, bleed settings, output intent), the print specification (press type: offset/digital/flexo, substrate, ink limit, required PDF standard), and any vendor-specific requirements.
Check each of the following categories and assign a status: PASS, WARN, or FAIL:
Document Setup: trim size matches press sheet, bleed is ≥3mm (offset) or ≥2mm (digital), slug area present if required, facing pages configured correctly.

Color: all placed images are CMYK or Grayscale (flag any RGB or LAB as FAIL), spot colors named correctly per Pantone/vendor spec, total ink coverage ≤ 300% (sheet-fed offset) or ≤ 280% (web offset), black text set to 100K not rich black (unless >24pt display text).
Images: all placed images ≥300 DPI at final print size (flag <300 DPI as FAIL, <250 DPI as WARN), images embedded or linked (warn on linked), no downsampled images from screen grabs.
Fonts: all fonts embedded, no missing fonts, no system fonts (Arial, Times New Roman flagged as WARN for professional print), no font size below 5pt (risk of fill-in).
Overprint & Trapping: black text set to overprint on, rich black backgrounds set to overprint, white objects not set to overprint (FAIL — will disappear).
PDF Export: PDF/X-1a for offset (CMYK only) or PDF/X-4 for digital/color-managed workflows, output intent profile embedded, compression settings appropriate (ZIP for images, no JPEG for 1-bit).

Produce a Preflight Report with: a Summary Table (category, status, issue count), a Findings List (each issue: category, element name or page number, severity, description, fix instruction), and a Go/No-Go verdict with the minimum fixes required before printing.

examples.title: Magazine cover preflight for offset print
examples.input: Document: A4 cover, 1 page, bleed 2mm, 2 placed images (1 RGB JPEG 220 DPI, 1 CMYK TIFF 350 DPI), fonts: Gill Sans (not embedded), Helvetica Neue (embedded). Color: CMYK job with 1 Pantone spot. Total ink at 340%. PDF/X-1a export. Vendor: sheet-fed offset, ink limit 300%.
examples.output:
Preflight Report — Magazine Cover

Summary Table:
| Category      | Status | Issues |
|---------------|--------|--------|
| Document Setup| WARN   | 1      |
| Color         | FAIL   | 2      |
| Images        | FAIL   | 2      |
| Fonts         | FAIL   | 1      |
| Overprint     | PASS   | 0      |
| PDF Export    | PASS   | 0      |

Findings:
[WARN] Document Setup / Bleed: 2mm bleed specified; sheet-fed offset requires minimum 3mm. Fix: extend bleed to 3mm in document setup.
[FAIL] Color / RGB Image: Placed image hero_photo.jpg is RGB. Must convert to CMYK before output. Fix: convert in Photoshop using the press ICC profile, re-link.
[FAIL] Color / Ink Density: Total ink coverage reaches 340% on page 1. Exceeds 300% limit. Fix: reduce shadow density in hero image — target max 85C 70M 70Y 90K in deepest shadows.
[FAIL] Images / Resolution: hero_photo.jpg is 220 DPI at final size. Minimum is 300 DPI. Fix: source a higher-resolution version or reduce placement size by 27%.
[WARN] Images / Linked File: hero_photo.jpg is linked, not embedded. Risk of missing file at RIP. Fix: embed before export or package with links.
[FAIL] Fonts / Missing Embed: Gill Sans is not embedded. Fix: embed all fonts in PDF export settings.

Go/No-Go: NO-GO. 4 FAIL items must be resolved before print. Minimum fixes: convert RGB image, reduce ink density, increase image resolution, embed Gill Sans.

# PrintPrepressChecker

## Purpose

PrintPrepressChecker acts as an expert preflight operator, auditing a document description or exported file manifest against a configurable print specification, and producing a Preflight Report that a designer can act on immediately.

## Instructions
1. Receive a preflight brief: the document description (dimensions, page count, color intent, image list with modes and resolutions, font list, bleed settings, output intent), the print specification (press type: offset/digital/flexo, substrate, ink limit, required PDF standard), and any vendor-specific requirements.
2. Check each of the following categories and assign a status: PASS, WARN, or FAIL:
3. Document Setup: trim size matches press sheet, bleed is ≥3mm (offset) or ≥2mm (digital), slug area present if required, facing pages configured correctly.
4. Color: all placed images are CMYK or Grayscale (flag any RGB or LAB as FAIL), spot colors named correctly per Pantone/vendor spec, total ink coverage ≤ 300% (sheet-fed offset) or ≤ 280% (web offset), black text set to 100K not rich black (unless >24pt display text).
5. Images: all placed images ≥300 DPI at final print size (flag <300 DPI as FAIL, <250 DPI as WARN), images embedded or linked (warn on linked), no downsampled images from screen grabs.
6. Fonts: all fonts embedded, no missing fonts, no system fonts (Arial, Times New Roman flagged as WARN for professional print), no font size below 5pt (risk of fill-in).
7. Overprint & Trapping: black text set to overprint on, rich black backgrounds set to overprint, white objects not set to overprint (FAIL — will disappear).
8. PDF Export: PDF/X-1a for offset (CMYK only) or PDF/X-4 for digital/color-managed workflows, output intent profile embedded, compression settings appropriate (ZIP for images, no JPEG for 1-bit).
9. Produce a Preflight Report with: a Summary Table (category, status, issue count), a Findings List (each issue: category, element name or page number, severity, description, fix instruction), and a Go/No-Go verdict with the minimum fixes required before printing.

## Prompt Template
```
Run a preflight check on the following document:

Document Description:
{{documentDescription}}

Print Specification:
{{printSpec}}

Vendor Requirements:
{{vendorRequirements}}

Instruction: Check all preflight categories (Document Setup, Color, Images, Fonts, Overprint, PDF Export). Produce the Preflight Report with Summary Table, Findings List, and Go/No-Go verdict.

## Examples

### Example 1: Magazine cover preflight for offset print

| Fonts         | FAIL   | 1      |
| Overprint     | PASS   | 0      |

[FAIL] Color / RGB Image: Placed image hero_photo.jpg is RGB. Must convert to CMYK before output. Fix: convert in Photoshop using the press ICC profile, re-link.
[FAIL] Color / Ink Density: Total ink coverage reaches 340% on page 1. Exceeds 300% limit. Fix: reduce shadow density in hero image — target max 85C 70M 70Y 90K in deepest shadows.
[FAIL] Images / Resolution: hero_photo.jpg is 220 DPI at final size. Minimum is 300 DPI. Fix: source a higher-resolution version or reduce placement size by 27%.
[WARN] Images / Linked File: hero_photo.jpg is linked, not embedded. Risk of missing file at RIP. Fix: embed before export or package with links.
[FAIL] Fonts / Missing Embed: Gill Sans is not embedded. Fix: embed all fonts in PDF export settings.

## Tests

### White overprint detected and flagged

skills.name: TypographySystemBuilder
skills.description: Constructs complete, consistent typographic systems for desktop publishing and UI applications — defining type scales, font pairings, hierarchy levels, spacing rules, and usage guidelines ready for implementation.

spec.name: TypographySystemBuilder
spec.description: Constructs complete, consistent typographic systems for desktop publishing and UI applications — defining type scales, font pairings, hierarchy levels, spacing rules, and usage guidelines ready for implementation.
spec.purpose: Eliminate typographic inconsistency in publishing and application projects by generating a complete, principled type system from first principles. A type system is not just a font choice — it is a coordinated set of rules governing every text element in a document or interface: what fonts are used, at what sizes, with what leading and tracking, in which contexts, and how they relate to each other. TypographySystemBuilder produces a system that is harmonious, accessible, and ready to paste directly into a design tool (InDesign character styles, Figma text styles, CSS custom properties, or a brand style guide).
instructions: Receive a type system brief: the medium (print, screen, or both), the content domain (editorial, corporate, technical, brand identity, UI), primary brand constraints (existing font family, color palette, tone), and target accessibility level (WCAG AA or AAA for screen, print readability standard for print).

Select or confirm font pairings: choose one display/heading font and one text/body font. Justify the pairing using contrast principles (serif+sans, geometric+humanist, etc.). For screen, confirm both fonts are available as web fonts or system fonts. For print, confirm licensing for commercial print use.

Build the modular type scale using a ratio (suggest: 1.25 Major Third for compact UI, 1.333 Perfect Fourth for editorial, 1.5 Perfect Fifth for display-heavy). Starting from the body size, calculate sizes for: Display, H1, H2, H3, H4, Body, Small, Caption, Label.

For each level, specify: size (pt for print, px/rem for screen), line height (leading), letter spacing (tracking), font weight, font style, text transform if any, and maximum line length (characters) for optimal readability.
Define spacing rules: the vertical rhythm unit (derived from body leading), space-above and space-below for each heading level, paragraph spacing, and list item spacing.

Produce the Type System Document with five sections: Font Pairing Rationale, Type Scale Table, Spacing Rules, Accessibility Checks (contrast ratios, minimum sizes, line length compliance), and Implementation Guide (CSS custom properties for screen, InDesign character styles for print, or both).
Flag any level that fails WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text) if a background color is provided.

examples.title: Editorial magazine type system (print + digital)

CSS Custom Properties:
```css
:root {
  --font-display: 'Playfair Display', Georgia, serif;
  --font-body: 'Source Serif 4', serif;

tests.input: Text color #999999, background #FFFFFF, accessibility target WCAG AA.
tests.expected: Type System flags #999/#FFF contrast ratio as 2.85:1 — FAILS AA for normal text (requires 4.5:1). Recommends darkening text to at minimum #767676 (4.54:1) and marks the Body level as FAIL with a corrected value.

Instruction: Select font pairing, build the modular scale, define spacing rules, check accessibility, and produce the full Type System Document with implementation code.
```

## Examples

### Example 1: Editorial magazine type system (print + digital)
**Input**: Medium: Print + Screen. Domain: Editorial magazine. Brand Fonts: Playfair Display (headings), none specified for body. Background: #FFFFFF. Text: #1A1A1A. Accessibility: WCAG AA. Base body: 16px screen / 10pt print. Scale: auto.

**Output**: Font Pairing: Playfair Display (Display, H1–H3) + Source Serif 4 (Body, H4, Caption). Rationale: Both are serif families — Playfair provides high-contrast display drama; Source Serif 4 offers optical-size optimization for body reading. Strong historical editorial pairing.

Type Scale (Perfect Fourth — 1.333):
| Level   | Screen      | Leading | Tracking | Weight |
|---------|-------------|---------|----------|--------|
| Display | 67px/4.2rem | 1.1     | −0.02em  | 700    |
| H1      | 50px/3.1rem | 1.15    | −0.01em  | 700    |
| H2      | 38px/2.4rem | 1.2     | 0        | 600    |
| H3      | 28px/1.75rem| 1.25    | 0        | 600    |
| H4      | 21px/1.3rem | 1.3     | 0        | 500    |
| Body    | 16px/1rem   | 1.6     | 0        | 400    |
| Small   | 12px/0.75rem| 1.5     | 0.01em   | 400    |
| Caption | 11px/0.69rem| 1.4     | 0.02em   | 400    |

Accessibility: Body contrast #1A1A1A on #FFF = 18.1:1 ✅ AAA. All levels pass AA.
Max line length: 65–75 chars for Body.

CSS Custom Properties:
```css
:root {
  --font-display: 'Playfair Display', Georgia, serif;
  --font-body: 'Source Serif 4', serif;
  --size-body: 1rem;
  --size-h1: 3.1rem;
  --leading-body: 1.6;
}
```

## Tests

### WCAG contrast failure flagged
**Input**: Text color #999999, background #FFFFFF, accessibility target WCAG AA.

**Expected**: Type System flags #999/#FFF contrast ratio as 2.85:1 — FAILS AA for normal text (requires 4.5:1). Recommends darkening text to at minimum #767676 (4.54:1) and marks the Body level as FAIL with a corrected value.

### Print and screen specs both generated
**Input**: Medium: both print and screen.

**Expected**: Type Scale Table includes both px/rem values (screen) and pt values (print) for every level. Implementation Guide includes both CSS custom properties and InDesign character style definitions.

# LayoutCompositor

## Purpose
Produce structured, professional-grade page layout specifications for desktop publishing projects — magazines, reports, books, brochures, presentations, and digital documents. The LayoutCompositor does not draw; it thinks spatially and expresses layouts as precise, implementation-ready specifications that a designer or DTP application (InDesign, Affinity Publisher, Scribus, QuarkXPress) can execute directly. It applies established layout principles — grid theory, optical margin alignment, the golden ratio, modular scale — to create layouts that are both beautiful and functionally sound.

## Instructions
1. Receive a layout brief: the document type (magazine spread, A4 report, brochure, book chapter, etc.), target dimensions, intended output medium (print or screen), content inventory (what text blocks, images, captions, and other elements must fit), and any brand or style constraints.
2. Define the grid: specify the number of columns, gutter width, margin sizes (top, bottom, inside, outside for facing pages), and baseline grid interval. Justify each choice relative to the content type and reading context.
3. Allocate zones: assign each content element (headline, body text, pull quote, image, caption, folio, sidebar) to a specific grid position using column/row notation. Identify primary, secondary, and tertiary hierarchy zones.
4. Specify typographic parameters for each zone: typeface family, weight, size, leading, tracking, alignment, and maximum line length in characters. Flag any zones where font size may need adjustment for accessibility (minimum 10pt for body print, 16px for screen body).
5. Define image placement rules: aspect ratio constraints, text-wrap mode (none, square, contour), bleed and trim marks for print, and resolution requirements (300 DPI for print, 72–96 PPI for screen).
6. Produce a Layout Spec document with four sections: Grid Definition, Zone Allocation Map (as an ASCII grid diagram), Element Specifications (a table: element name, grid position, typographic spec, image spec), and Implementation Notes (file setup instructions for the target DTP application).
7. If critiquing an existing layout, identify: hierarchy violations (elements competing for the same visual weight), white space imbalances, widows and orphans, axis misalignments, and print-safety issues (elements too close to bleed or trim).

## Prompt Template
```
Produce a layout specification for the following brief:

Document Type: {{documentType}}
Dimensions: {{dimensions}}
Output Medium: {{medium}}
Content Inventory: {{contentInventory}}
Brand Constraints: {{brandConstraints}}
Target DTP Application: {{dtpApp}}

- Baseline grid: 14pt (aligned to body leading).

Zone Allocation Map:
```
| IMG (cols 1–6, rows 1–3) FULL BLEED     |
| HEAD (cols 1–4) | CALLOUT A (cols 5–6) |
| BODY col 1–2   | BODY col 3–4 | BODY 5–6|
| CALLOUT B (cols 1–2) | FOLIO (col 6)  |
```

Element Specifications:
| Element   | Grid Position     | Type Spec                        |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| Headline  | Cols 1–4, Row 2   | Helvetica Neue Bold, 28/32pt, −20 tracking |
| Body Text | Cols 1–6, Row 3   | Helvetica Neue Regular, 9.5/14pt, 0 tracking, justified |
| Callout A | Cols 5–6, Row 2   | Helvetica Neue Medium, 18/22pt, navy fill box |
| Callout B | Cols 1–2, Row 4   | Helvetica Neue Light, 14/18pt, left-aligned |
| Folio     | Col 6, Row 4      | Helvetica Neue Regular, 8pt, right-aligned |

Image: Full bleed, 300 DPI minimum, CMYK, extend 3mm past trim on all bleeding edges.

skills.markdown:
# Present

## Purpose
Provide a clear, grounded snapshot of the current moment so that nothing important is lost at session boundaries and both the Diary and Roadmap can be updated with accurate, high-fidelity information. The Present is the source of truth for what is actually happening — not what was planned or what is remembered.

## Instructions
1. On each invocation, receive a raw session dump: notes, messages, decisions, actions, thoughts in any format.
2. Extract and organize into five Present fields: Active Focus (what is being worked on right now), Decisions In Flight (decisions started but not yet resolved), Open Loops (tasks or threads started but not closed), Immediate Next Actions (the very next 1-3 steps), and Signal (the current energy, mood, momentum, or friction level of the session).
3. Write in the present tense — is, are, happening, working — not was or will.
4. Flag anything that should immediately update the Diary (a lesson learned, a decision made) with [→ DIARY].
5. Flag anything that should update the Roadmap (a new risk, a milestone reached, a future decision crystallized) with [→ ROADMAP].
6. Keep the Present snapshot concise — it is a live readout, not a full narrative. Aim for scannable density.
7. At session close, produce a Present Closing Summary — a single paragraph handoff note for the next session.

## Prompt Template
```
Capture the Present context from the following session input:

Session marker: {{sessionMarker}}
Raw session input (notes, chat, decisions, actions): {{rawInput}}

Existing Present snapshot (if any): {{existingPresent}}

Instruction: Extract Active Focus, Decisions In Flight, Open Loops, Immediate Next Actions, and Signal. Flag [→ DIARY] and [→ ROADMAP] items. Produce a Closing Summary if this is a session end.
```

## Examples

### Example 1: Mid-session context capture
**Input**: Session 3. Currently debugging the auth token expiry logic. Decided to use sliding window expiry but have not implemented it yet. Still need to write tests for the refresh endpoint. Next: finish the sliding window implementation, then write tests. Team feels a bit stuck — third day on this issue.

**Output**: **Present Snapshot — Session 3**

**Active Focus**: Debugging and resolving auth token expiry logic — specifically the sliding window expiry approach.

**Decisions In Flight**:
- Sliding window expiry selected over fixed expiry [→ DIARY: DECISION] — implementation pending

**Open Loops**:
- Refresh endpoint tests not yet written
- Token expiry edge cases (concurrent requests) not yet explored

**Immediate Next Actions**:
1. Implement sliding window expiry in the token service
2. Write unit tests for refresh endpoint
3. Document the expiry strategy in the API spec

**Signal**: Momentum is low — three days on the same issue is creating friction. Consider a timebox: if not resolved in 2 hours, escalate or pair program. [→ DIARY: SHIFT]

### Example 2: Session closing summary
**Input**: End of Session 3. We got the sliding window working. Tests are written. Moving to core endpoints next session.

**Output**: **Present Closing Summary — Session 3**
Auth token expiry is resolved — sliding window implemented and tested. All open loops from the auth module are closed. [→ DIARY: MILESTONE] [→ ROADMAP: mark Auth module DONE, advance to Core Endpoint Suite]. Next session begins with a clean slate on the core endpoint work. Team energy should recover with this milestone behind us.

## Tests

### Flags diary and roadmap routing correctly
**Input**: Just decided to switch from PostgreSQL to SQLite for local dev. Learned that our ORM supports both transparently.

**Expected**: The decision is flagged [→ DIARY: DECISION] and the lesson about ORM compatibility is flagged [→ DIARY: LESSON]. No Roadmap flags unless the switch affects future milestones.

### Produces closing summary at session end
**Input**: Session end. Completed the search endpoint. Still need to do pagination. Energy is good.

**Expected**: Output includes a Present Closing Summary paragraph suitable as a handoff note, with [→ DIARY] and [→ ROADMAP] flags for the search endpoint completion.

spec.name: Roadmap
spec.description: Maintains a living, forward-looking context map — tracking goals, intentions, upcoming decisions, anticipated obstacles, and the north star that orients all current work.
spec.purpose: Keep the future visible and navigable. The Roadmap is not a static plan — it is a continuously updated projection that bends as the present changes. It holds the horizon steady while remaining honest about what is uncertain, what has shifted, and what must be re-prioritized.
instructions: On each invocation, receive the current session context and any existing Roadmap state.

Maintain four sections: North Star (the unchanging purpose or end goal), Horizon (next 3-5 milestones in order), Watch List (known risks, dependencies, and open decisions ahead), and Uncertainty Fog (things expected to happen but not yet clear enough to plan).
When new information from the present changes the future, update the affected Roadmap section and annotate the change with a [REVISED] tag and a one-line reason.
Mark milestones as [UPCOMING], [AT RISK], or [BLOCKED] based on current context.
Never remove a Watch List item — mark it [RESOLVED] when it clears, so the history of what was anticipated is preserved.
Keep language forward-facing and active — use will, intend to, plan to, expect — not passive or vague phrasing.
Output the full Roadmap as a structured document every time it is updated.

# Roadmap

## Purpose
Keep the future visible and navigable. The Roadmap is not a static plan — it is a continuously updated projection that bends as the present changes. It holds the horizon steady while remaining honest about what is uncertain, what has shifted, and what must be re-prioritized.

## Instructions
1. On each invocation, receive the current session context and any existing Roadmap state.
2. Maintain four sections: North Star (the unchanging purpose or end goal), Horizon (next 3-5 milestones in order), Watch List (known risks, dependencies, and open decisions ahead), and Uncertainty Fog (things expected to happen but not yet clear enough to plan).
3. When new information from the present changes the future, update the affected Roadmap section and annotate the change with a [REVISED] tag and a one-line reason.
4. Mark milestones as [UPCOMING], [AT RISK], or [BLOCKED] based on current context.
5. Never remove a Watch List item — mark it [RESOLVED] when it clears, so the history of what was anticipated is preserved.
6. Keep language forward-facing and active — use will, intend to, plan to, expect — not passive or vague phrasing.
7. Output the full Roadmap as a structured document every time it is updated.

## Prompt Template
```
Update the Roadmap with the following current context:

Session marker: {{sessionMarker}}
What changed this session: {{sessionChanges}}
New information affecting the future: {{newInformation}}
Completed milestones: {{completed}}
New risks or blockers identified: {{risks}}

Existing Roadmap:
{{existingRoadmap}}

Instruction: Revise the Roadmap, annotating any changes with [REVISED] and a reason.
```

## Examples

### Example 1: Initial Roadmap for an app launch
**Input**: North star: launch a publicly available API by end of Q3. Sessions so far: 1. Completed: project scaffold. Next: auth module, core endpoints, docs, beta.

**Output**: **🎯 North Star**
Ship a publicly available, documented API by end of Q3 that handles 1,000 concurrent requests reliably.

**📅 Horizon**

2. [UPCOMING] Core endpoint suite (CRUD + search)

- Post-launch support model undefined

### Example 2: Roadmap update after a blocker is discovered
**Input**: Session 4. The OAuth provider we planned to use has a 6-week approval wait. Auth module is at risk. We may need to ship with email/password only first.

**Output**: **🎯 North Star**
Ship a publicly available, documented API by end of Q3. (unchanged)

**📅 Horizon**
1. [AT RISK] Auth module [REVISED] OAuth dependency has 6-week wait — will ship email/password auth first; OAuth integration moves to post-launch
2. [UPCOMING] Core endpoint suite
3. [UPCOMING] OpenAPI documentation
4. [UPCOMING] Closed beta
5. [UPCOMING] Public launch

**👁️ Watch List**
- OAuth provider approval wait: 6 weeks [ACTIVE BLOCKER]

spec.name: Diary
spec.description: Maintains a living record of past session context — capturing what happened, what was decided, what was learned, and what emotions or friction arose. Each entry is enriched over time as patterns and themes emerge from accumulated experience.
spec.purpose: Preserve the meaningful thread of what has already happened so that past experience informs the present and future rather than being lost. The Diary does not just log events — it reflects on them, surfaces recurring themes, and distills the lessons gained along the way.
instructions: On each invocation, receive the current session summary and any existing Diary entries.
Identify what happened: decisions made, milestones reached, obstacles encountered, knowledge gained, and emotional or motivational shifts.
Write a new Diary entry in first-person past tense, timestamped with a session marker (e.g. Session 3, Sprint 2-Day 4).
After three or more entries exist, append an Emerging Themes section that synthesizes cross-entry patterns — recurring blockers, consistent wins, evolving priorities.

# Diary

## Purpose
Preserve the meaningful thread of what has already happened so that past experience informs the present and future rather than being lost. The Diary does not just log events — it reflects on them, surfaces recurring themes, and distills the lessons gained along the way.

## Instructions
1. On each invocation, receive the current session summary and any existing Diary entries.
2. Identify what happened: decisions made, milestones reached, obstacles encountered, knowledge gained, and emotional or motivational shifts.
3. Write a new Diary entry in first-person past tense, timestamped with a session marker (e.g. Session 3, Sprint 2-Day 4).
4. After three or more entries exist, append an Emerging Themes section that synthesizes cross-entry patterns — recurring blockers, consistent wins, evolving priorities.
5. Tag each entry with: [DECISION], [LESSON], [OBSTACLE], [MILESTONE], [SHIFT] to make the log scannable.
6. Never delete old entries — only enrich them with retrospective annotations when new context reveals their deeper significance.
7. Output the full Diary in chronological order with the newest entry last.

## Prompt Template
```
Update the Diary with the following session context:

Session marker: {{sessionMarker}}
What happened this session: {{sessionSummary}}
Decisions made: {{decisions}}
Obstacles or friction: {{obstacles}}
Lessons or insights: {{lessons}}

Existing Diary:
{{existingDiary}}

Instruction: Write a new enriched entry and append it. Update Emerging Themes if three or more entries exist.
```

## Examples

### Example 1: First diary entry for a software project
**Input**: Session 1. We decided to build the API in FastAPI rather than Django REST. Hit an environment issue with Python versions. Learned that the team has stronger async experience than expected.

[LESSON] Integration testing consistently takes 40-60% longer than estimated. Build this multiplier into future planning.
[SHIFT] Team energy noticeably elevated after seeing the demo — visibility of progress is a morale lever worth scheduling deliberately.
---

**✨ Emerging Themes (Sessions 1-3)**
- Environment and tooling friction front-loads pain — invest in setup rigour early.
- The team performs above expectation when given autonomy on tech choices.
- Integration testing is the consistent underestimate — adjust sprint planning.

examples.title: Developer newsletter issue on AI coding tools
examples.input: Newsletter: The Stack. Topic: How AI coding tools are changing code review. Audience: senior developers. Voice: direct, no-hype. CTA: reply with opinion.
examples.output:
**Subject line options**:
1. AI is rewriting code review
2. What AI gets wrong about your PRs
3. 3 ways Copilot changes code review

**Preview text**: The good, the bad, and what still needs a human.

**Intro**: Hey — short issue this week, but an important one. I have spent the last month using four different AI code review tools on real PRs, and I have thoughts.

**Main content**:
AI code review is getting genuinely useful, but not in the way the marketing suggests.

The tools are great at catching obvious bugs, style inconsistencies, and missing error handling. Where they fall flat is context. They do not know your team conventions, your technical debt story, or why that particular hack exists in the first place.

The real value I have found: use AI review as a first pass to catch the low-hanging fruit, so human reviewers can spend their limited attention on architecture, logic, and team knowledge transfer.

That reframing — AI as a first-pass filter, not a replacement — makes the tools far less threatening and far more practical.

**Links this week**:
- GitHub Copilot code review is now GA — worth enabling on your org to form your own opinion.
- A thoughtful thread on why AI cannot replace the social function of code review.

**CTA**: Hit reply — are you using AI in your review workflow? I am collecting data for a follow-up issue.

spec.name: SQL Query Optimizer
spec.description: Analyzes slow or inefficient SQL queries and rewrites them for better performance, explaining each optimization with its expected impact.
spec.purpose: Help developers and DBAs identify and fix slow queries without needing deep database internals expertise.
instructions: Read the provided SQL query and any accompanying schema or EXPLAIN output.
Identify performance anti-patterns: missing indexes, SELECT *, correlated subqueries, implicit type casts, unnecessary DISTINCT, Cartesian joins, etc.
Rewrite the query for performance and explain each change in plain English.
Suggest indexes to create (with CREATE INDEX DDL) and explain why each helps.
Estimate the relative improvement where possible (e.g. full-table scan eliminated).
Note any trade-offs — e.g. index write overhead or changed result semantics.

examples.title: N+1 pattern elimination
examples.input: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = (SELECT id FROM users WHERE email = a@b.com);
examples.output:
**Issue**: Correlated subquery forces the inner SELECT to run once per candidate row.
**Optimized**:
```sql
SELECT o.*
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN users u ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE u.email = a@b.com;
```
**Index suggestion**:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders(user_id);
```
**Impact**: Reduces query to a single indexed JOIN instead of a full scan per row.

tests.name: Replaces SELECT * with explicit columns
tests.input: SELECT * FROM products WHERE category = electronics;
tests.expected: Recommends replacing SELECT * with explicit column list and suggests an index on category.

tests.name: Suggests covering index
tests.input: SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE status = 1 ORDER BY created_at DESC;
tests.expected: Suggests a covering index on (status, created_at, name, email) to avoid a filesort and table lookup.

skills.markdown:
# SQL Query Optimizer

## Purpose
Help developers and DBAs identify and fix slow queries without needing deep database internals expertise.

## Instructions
1. Read the provided SQL query and any accompanying schema or EXPLAIN output.
2. Identify performance anti-patterns: missing indexes, SELECT *, correlated subqueries, implicit type casts, unnecessary DISTINCT, Cartesian joins, etc.
3. Rewrite the query for performance and explain each change in plain English.
4. Suggest indexes to create (with CREATE INDEX DDL) and explain why each helps.
5. Estimate the relative improvement where possible (e.g. full-table scan eliminated).
6. Note any trade-offs — e.g. index write overhead or changed result semantics.

## Prompt Template
```
Optimize the following SQL query:

```sql
{{query}}
```

Database: {{dbEngine}}
Table schemas (optional):
{{schema}}

EXPLAIN output (optional):
{{explainOutput}}
```

## Examples

### Example 1: N+1 pattern elimination
**Input**: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = (SELECT id FROM users WHERE email = a@b.com);

**Output**: **Issue**: Correlated subquery forces the inner SELECT to run once per candidate row.
**Optimized**:
```sql
SELECT o.*
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN users u ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE u.email = a@b.com;
```
**Index suggestion**:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders(user_id);
```
**Impact**: Reduces query to a single indexed JOIN instead of a full scan per row.

## Tests

### Replaces SELECT * with explicit columns
**Input**: SELECT * FROM products WHERE category = electronics;

**Expected**: Recommends replacing SELECT * with explicit column list and suggests an index on category.

### Suggests covering index
**Input**: SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE status = 1 ORDER BY created_at DESC;

**Expected**: Suggests a covering index on (status, created_at, name, email) to avoid a filesort and table lookup.

skills.markdown:
# Git Commit Message Writer

## Purpose
Keep git history clean, searchable, and automatically changelog-ready with consistent Conventional Commits formatting.

## Instructions
1. Determine the correct type: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, build, ci, chore, or revert.
2. Identify the scope (affected module, component, or file) when discernible.
3. Write a subject line under 72 characters in the imperative mood (add, fix, remove — not added, fixed, removed).
4. Add a body paragraph explaining motivation and what changed when the change is non-trivial.
5. Add a BREAKING CHANGE footer when the change breaks backward compatibility.
6. Output only the commit message — no surrounding commentary.

## Prompt Template
```
Write a Git commit message for the following change:

{{changeDescription}}

Diff (optional):
```diff
{{diff}}
```
```

## Examples

### Example 1: New feature
**Input**: Added a dark mode toggle to the settings page. It stores the user preference in localStorage.

**Output**: feat(settings): add dark mode toggle with localStorage persistence

Introduce a dark mode toggle on the settings page. Preference is stored
in localStorage under `theme` and applied on load to prevent FOUC.

### Example 2: Breaking API change
**Input**: Renamed the /api/v1/users endpoint to /api/v2/members. Existing clients will break.

**Output**: feat(api)!: rename /v1/users to /v2/members

Migrate the users resource to the v2 API surface under the members path.

BREAKING CHANGE: /api/v1/users is removed. Update all clients to use /api/v2/members.

## Tests

### Uses imperative mood in subject
**Input**: Removed the deprecated getUserById function from the auth module.

**Expected**: Subject line uses remove (not removed) in the imperative mood.

### Adds BREAKING CHANGE footer
**Input**: Dropped support for Node.js 14. Minimum is now Node.js 18.

**Expected**: Commit message includes a BREAKING CHANGE footer noting the minimum Node.js version change.

Break down complex development tasks into safe, ordered implementation steps.

## Instructions
1. Provide a clear and concise product request, bug report, or development idea.
2. The skill will generate an execution plan for an AI coding agent.
3. The plan will include implementation phases, test plan, acceptance criteria, and rollback considerations.

## Prompt Template
```
Please provide a product request, bug report, or vague development idea for the AI coding agent to convert into an execution plan. The plan should include {{input}}.
```

## Examples
### Example 1: Product Request
**Input**

```
Create a login feature for our web application.
```

**Output**

```
Clarified objective: Implement a secure login feature for the web application.
Assumptions: The application uses a secure authentication protocol.
Files/directories to inspect: /app/login, /app/auth.
Implementation phases: Phase 1: Design login UI, Phase 2: Implement login API, Phase 3: Integrate login with authentication protocol.
Test plan: Unit test login API, Integration test login UI.
Acceptance criteria: User can log in successfully, User can log out successfully.
Risks and edge cases: Password reset, Account lockout.
Final instruction prompt: Implement the login feature using the provided design and API specifications.
```

### Example 2: Bug Report
**Input**

```
The login feature is not working correctly.
```

**Output**

```
Clarified objective: Fix the login feature bug.
Assumptions: The bug is related to authentication protocol.
Files/directories to inspect: /app/login, /app/auth.
Implementation phases: Phase 1: Investigate login API, Phase 2: Fix login API, Phase 3: Test login API.
Test plan: Unit test login API, Integration test login UI.
Acceptance criteria: User can log in successfully, User can log out successfully.
Risks and edge cases: Password reset, Account lockout.
Final instruction prompt: Fix the login feature bug using the provided debug information.
```
